name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
وَكَذَٰلِكَ نَجْزِى مَنْ أَسْرَفَ وَلَمْ يُؤْمِنۢ بِـَٔايَـٰتِ رَبِّهِۦ ۚ وَلَعَذَابُ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ أَشَدُّ وَأَبْقَىٰٓ (١٢٧)
```

```english
Thus do We requite those who transgress and do not believe in the signs of their Lord, and the punishment of the Hereafter is severer and more lasting. (127)
```

```arabic
أَفَلَمْ يَهْدِ لَهُمْ كَمْ أَهْلَكْنَا قَبْلَهُم مِّنَ ٱلْقُرُونِ يَمْشُونَ فِى مَسَـٰكِنِهِمْ ۗ إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَـٰتٍ لِّأُو۟لِى ٱلنُّهَىٰ (١٢٨)
```

```english
Does it not dawn upon them how many generations We have destroyed before them, amid [the ruins of] whose dwellings they walk? There are indeed signs in this for those who have reason. (128)
```

```arabic
وَلَوْلَا كَلِمَةٌ سَبَقَتْ مِن رَّبِّكَ لَكَانَ لِزَامًا وَأَجَلٌ مُّسَمًّى (١٢٩)
```

```english
Were it not for a prior decree of your Lord and a specified time, [their doom] would have attended [their guilt]. (129)
```

```arabic
فَٱصْبِرْ عَلَىٰ مَا يَقُولُونَ وَسَبِّحْ بِحَمْدِ رَبِّكَ قَبْلَ طُلُوعِ ٱلشَّمْسِ وَقَبْلَ غُرُوبِهَا ۖ وَمِنْ ءَانَآئِ ٱلَّيْلِ فَسَبِّحْ وَأَطْرَافَ ٱلنَّهَارِ لَعَلَّكَ تَرْضَىٰ (١٣٠)
```

```english
So be patient with what they say, and celebrate the praise of your Lord before the rising of the sun and before the sunset, and glorify Him in watches of the night and at the day’s ends, that you may be pleased. (130)
```

```arabic
وَلَا تَمُدَّنَّ عَيْنَيْكَ إِلَىٰ مَا مَتَّعْنَا بِهِۦٓ أَزْوَٰجًا مِّنْهُمْ زَهْرَةَ ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا لِنَفْتِنَهُمْ فِيهِ ۚ وَرِزْقُ رَبِّكَ خَيْرٌ وَأَبْقَىٰ (١٣١)
```

```english
Do not extend your glance toward what We have provided certain groups of them as a glitter of the life of this world, so that We may test them thereby. The provision of your Lord is better and more lasting. (131)
```

```arabic
وَأْمُرْ أَهْلَكَ بِٱلصَّلَوٰةِ وَٱصْطَبِرْ عَلَيْهَا ۖ لَا نَسْـَٔلُكَ رِزْقًا ۖ نَّحْنُ نَرْزُقُكَ ۗ وَٱلْعَـٰقِبَةُ لِلتَّقْوَىٰ (١٣٢)
```

```english
And bid your family to prayer and be steadfast in maintaining it. We do not ask any provision of you: it is We who provide for you, and to Godwariness belongs the ultimate outcome [in the Hereafter]. (132)
```

```arabic
وَقَالُوا۟ لَوْلَا يَأْتِينَا بِـَٔايَةٍ مِّن رَّبِّهِۦٓ ۚ أَوَلَمْ تَأْتِهِم بَيِّنَةُ مَا فِى ٱلصُّحُفِ ٱلْأُولَىٰ (١٣٣)
```

```english
They say, ‘Why does he not bring us a sign from his Lord?’ Has there not come to them a clear proof in that which is in the former scriptures? (133)
```

```arabic
وَلَوْ أَنَّآ أَهْلَكْنَـٰهُم بِعَذَابٍ مِّن قَبْلِهِۦ لَقَالُوا۟ رَبَّنَا لَوْلَآ أَرْسَلْتَ إِلَيْنَا رَسُولًا فَنَتَّبِعَ ءَايَـٰتِكَ مِن قَبْلِ أَن نَّذِلَّ وَنَخْزَىٰ (١٣٤)
```

```english
Had We destroyed them with a punishment before it, they would have surely said, ‘Our Lord! Why did You not send us an apostle so that we might have followed Your signs before we were abased and disgraced?’ (134)
```

```arabic
قُلْ كُلٌّ مُّتَرَبِّصٌ فَتَرَبَّصُوا۟ ۖ فَسَتَعْلَمُونَ مَنْ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلصِّرَٰطِ ٱلسَّوِىِّ وَمَنِ ٱهْتَدَىٰ (١٣٥)
```

```english
Say, ‘Everyone [of us] is waiting. So wait! Soon you will know who are the people of the right path, and who is [rightly] guided.’ (135)
```

## (Commentary)

This section contains miscellaneous matters of warning, promise, argument, ruling, and consolation for the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله وسلم), all branching from what has preceded in the Surah.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **"And thus do We reward him who is excessive and does not believe in the signs of his Lord; and the punishment of the Hereafter is severer and more lasting."** (20:127). **Excess (الإسراف)** is the crossing of limits. It appears that the "and" (الواو) in His saying "And thus" (وَكَذلِكَ) is for the commencement of a new sentence (للاستيناف), and the reference is to the aforementioned seizing of the one who turned away from the remembrance of Allah and forgot the signs of his Lord. Indeed, such a person has transgressed the limit of servitude (العبودية) and disbelieved in the signs of his Lord; thus, his reward is the reward of one who forgot the signs of his Lord and abandoned them after they were entrusted to him, while turning away from His remembrance.

His saying: **"and the punishment of the Hereafter is severer and more lasting"** means it is more intense than the punishment of this world. This is because it encompasses the inner reality of man just as it does his outer form, and because it is perpetual and never ceases.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **"Does it not guide them [to see] how many generations We have destroyed before them, in whose dwellings they walk?..."** (20:128) etc. It appears that "guide" (يَهْدِ) carries the implied meaning of "clarify" (يبين). The meaning is: Has the path of reflection and faith in the signs not been clarified for them by the frequency of Our destruction of the generations that preceded them, while they walk in their dwellings? This is similar to how the people of Mecca, in their travels, would pass by the dwellings of *‘Ad* in the sand-dunes (الأحقاف) of Yemen, the dwellings of *Thamud* and the Dwellers of the Grove (أصحاب الأيكة) in the Levant, and the dwellings of the people of Lot in Palestine. **"Indeed in that are signs for those who possess intellect (لِأُولِي النُّهى)"**—meaning the masters of reason.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **"And were it not for a word that had already gone forth from your Lord, it would have been inevitable, and [for] a term already set."** (20:129). The requirement of the preceding context is that "inevitable" (لِزاماً) carries the meaning of "necessity" or "adherence" (الملازمة); both are verbal nouns of the verb "to adhere" (لازم يلازم). The verbal noun here is intended to carry the meaning of the active participle (اسم الفاعل). Accordingly, the subject of the verb "was" (كان) is the pronoun referring back to the "destruction" mentioned in the previous verse. Furthermore, His saying: "and a term already set" (وَأَجَلٌ مُسَمًّى) is coordinated with "a word that had already gone forth" (كَلِمَةٌ سَبَقَتْ). The underlying structure is: "Were it not for a word that had already gone forth from your Lord and a term already set, destruction would have been inevitable (ملازماً) for them since they were excessive and did not believe in the signs of their Lord."

Some have suggested that "inevitable" (لِزام) is the name of an instrument (اسم آلة), like a belt (حزام) or a stirrup (ركاب), while others suggested it is the plural of "necessary" (لازم), like "standing" (قيام) is the plural of "one who stands" (قائم). However, neither of these two meanings aligns well with the context.

Regarding His saying: **"And were it not for a word that had already gone forth from your Lord,"** this "word" has been repeated by Him, the Glorified, regarding the Children of Israel and others in several places of His speech, such as His saying: **"And were it not for a word that had already gone forth from your Lord, it would have been decided between them"** (10:19, 11:110, 41:45). He has limited it by a set term in His saying: **"And were it not for a word that had already gone forth from your Lord until a term already set, it would have been decided between them"** (42:14). It has already been mentioned in the commentary (تفسير) of Surah Yunus and Surah Hud that what is meant by it is the "word" by which judgment was passed at the time of Adam's descent to the earth, such as His saying: **"and in the earth there shall be an abode for you and a provision for a while"** (7:24).

Thus, people are safe from destruction and the punishment of extirpation (عذاب الاستئصال) despite their excess and disbelief during the period between their settlement on earth and their set term—unless a Messenger comes to them and judgment is passed between them. The Exalted said: **"There is a messenger for every nation: so when their messenger comes, judgment is made between them with justice, and they are not wronged"** (10:47). To this refers the punishment of extirpation regarding the suggested signs if they do not believe in them after they have come. The condition of this nation (الأمة) is like that of all other nations regarding safety from the punishment of extirpation due to a prior promise from Allah. As for the judgment between them and the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله وسلم), Allah has delayed it for a period, as was previously derived from His saying: "There is a messenger for every nation..." in Surah Yunus.

Some have suggested that the "word" refers to a specific promise to this nation to delay the punishment from them until the Day of Resurrection. However, it was mentioned in the commentary of Surah Yunus that the apparent meaning of the verses contradicts this; rather, His speech indicates its delay for a period, as mentioned.

Similar in its invalidity is the view of others who say that the "word" refers to the decree of punishment for the people of Badr among them by the sword, and the "set term" refers to the remainder of the disbelievers of Mecca—and this is as you see [unfounded].

Regarding His saying: **"and a term already set (وَأَجَلٌ مُسَمًّى),"** it was previously explained in the commentary of the beginning of Surah al-An'am that the "set term" is the specific time designated by name (التسمية) which is neither bypassed nor failed, as the Exalted said: **"No community can advance its term, nor can they defer it"** (15:5). Some mentioned that the "set term" refers to the Day of Resurrection, while others said the "set term" is the "word" that went forth from Allah, making the coordination of "term" with "word" an explanatory coordination (عطف تفسير). Neither of these two views is to be relied upon due to the lack of evidence.

The summary of the verse's meaning is: Were it not for the word that had already gone forth from your Lord—and the addition of "Lord" to the second-person pronoun is an honoring and support for the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله وسلم)—which decrees the delay of their punishment, and the set term which designates its time within that period of delay, destruction would have been inseparable from them immediately upon their excess and disbelief.

From this, it becomes clear that the combination of the "word that went forth" and the "set term" constitutes a single complete cause (سبب واحد تام) for delaying the punishment from them, not that each of them is an independent cause for that, as many [commentators] have chosen.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **"So be patient with what they say, and celebrate the praise of your Lord before the rising of the sun and before its setting..."** (20:130) etc. He commands him to be patient with what they say, branching this from what preceded. It is as if it were said: Since it is from the decree of Allah to delay their punishment and not to hasten vengeance upon them for what they say, nothing remains for you but to be patient, being pleased with what Allah has decreed, and to sanctify Him from the words of polytheism (الشرك) they utter and the evil they confront you with. You should praise Him for the effects of His decree that you face, for it is nothing but beautiful. Therefore, be patient with what they say and celebrate the praise of your Lord, that you may be pleased.

His saying: **"and celebrate the praise of your Lord"** means sanctify Him while being clothed in His praise and commendation. For these events, which are difficult to bear and require patience, have a relation to their actors and are nothing but evil from which Allah must be sanctified; yet they also have a relation to Him by His permission, and in this relation, they are beautiful, resulting only in general benefits that rectify the cosmic order. Thus, Allah should be praised and commended for them.

His saying: **"before the rising of the sun and before its setting"** are two temporal adverbs (ظرفان) related to His saying: "and celebrate the praise of your Lord."

His saying: **"and during the hours of the night, celebrate [His praise]"**—this sentence is similar to His saying: **"and Me alone should you fear"** (2:40). "Hours" (الآناء) is the plural of *iny* (إني) or *inw* (إنو) with a *kasrah* on the *hamzah*, meaning "time." The "from" (مِنْ) denotes partitivity (التبعيض). The preposition and its object are related to His saying: "so celebrate" (فَسَبِّحْ), indicating a temporal adverb within its meaning related to the verb. The underlying structure is: "and during some hours of the night, celebrate [His praise] therein."

His saying: **"and at the ends of the day"** is in the accusative case due to the removal of the preposition (منصوب بنزع الخافض), as they have mentioned, and it is coordinated with His saying: "and during [some] hours" (وَمِنْ آناءِ). The underlying structure is: "and celebrate at the ends of the day." Is what is meant by the "ends of the day" the time before sunrise and before sunset, or something else? The words of the exegetes differ on this, and we shall point to it.

The "celebration" (التسبيح) mentioned in the verse is absolute; there is no indication from the wording itself that it refers to the daily obligatory prayers (الفرائض اليومية). Some exegetes leaned toward this, but most insisted that "celebration" here means the prayer (الصلاة), following what was narrated from some of the early scholars like Qatadah and others.

They said: The totality of the verse indicates the command for the five daily prayers. His saying: "before the rising of the sun" is the Dawn prayer (صلاة الصبح); His saying: "and before its setting" is the Afternoon prayer (صلاة العصر); His saying: "and during the hours of the night" are the Sunset and Night prayers (صلاتا المغرب والعشاء); and His saying: "and at the ends of the day" is the Noon prayer (صلاة الظهر).

The meaning of it being at the "ends of the day," even though it is in the middle after the sun's decline (الزوال), is that if the day were halved, there would be two halves: the first and the last. The Noon prayer is in the first part of the second half; thus, it is at the "end" of the first half because the end of the first half reaches a point that connects to its time. It is also at the "end" of the second half because it begins from a point that is its time. So its time, in its singularity, is one "end" for the first half in one respect, and an "end" for the second half in another respect; thus, it is two "ends" (طرفان اثنان) conceptually.

As for the use of "ends" (الأطراف)—in the plural form—for its time when it is only two ends conceptually, it is because the plural may be used for two, even though the most well-known rule in the Arabic language is that the minimum for a plural is three. It was also said: "day" (النهار) refers to the genus, so in every individual day there are two ends, thus making them "ends" (plural). The debate among them regarding this justification, in terms of objection and response, has been lengthy.

However, in fairness, the basis of this justification is an affectation (تعسف) far removed from [natural] understanding. Sound taste (الذوق السليم)—after all is said and done—refuses to call the middle of the day the "ends of the day" based on imaginary assumptions and considerations for which there is no necessity in the context of communication, nor is it a matter that taste accepts or finds agreeable.

As for those who said that "celebration" and "praise" refer to something other than the obligatory prayers—meaning absolute glorification and praise, either by remembering His sanctity and commendation in the heart, or by saying things like "Glory be to Allah" (سبحان الله) and "Praise be to Allah" (الحمد لله) with the tongue, or both—they said: What is meant by "before the rising of the sun," "before its setting," and "the hours of the night" are the morning, the afternoon, and the times of the night; and "the ends of the day" are the morning and the afternoon.

Regarding the necessary use of "ends" (الأطراف), which is a plural, for the morning and afternoon, which are two, they answered with the same as the previous view regarding the minimum of the plural being two. Regarding the necessary repetition by mentioning the morning and afternoon glorification twice, some accepted this, saying it is for emphasis and to show extra care for glorification in these two times. It appears from some that "the ends" refers to the morning, the afternoon, and the middle of the day.

You are well aware that this is subject to similar objections as the previous view, with slight variation. The entire problem arises from His saying: "and at the ends of the day" (وَأَطْرافَ النَّهارِ) in terms of its applicability to the middle of the day or the morning and afternoon.

What can be said is that His saying: "and at the ends of the day" is a "comitative object" (مفعول معه) and not a temporal adverb with an implied "in" (في), though the exegetes have not mentioned this as far as I recall. What is meant by the "ends of the day" is the time before sunrise and before sunset, considering them as two expansive times, each having parts, every part of which is an "end" (طرف) relative to the middle of the day. Thus, it is correct to call them the "ends of the day" (أطراف النهار) just as it is correct to call them the "two ends of the day" (طرفي النهار). This is like how the time before sunrise is called the "beginning of the day" (أول النهار) considering its unity, and the "beginnings of the day" (أوائل النهار) considering its division into parts; and the time before sunset is called the "end of the day" (آخر النهار) and the "ends of the day" (أواخر النهار).

Thus, the meaning of the verse resolves to something like our saying: "And celebrate the praise of your Lord before the rising of the sun and before its setting—which are the ends of the day—and during some times of the night, celebrate therein along with the ends of the day in which you were commanded to celebrate."

If you ask: How can "the ends of the day" (أَطْرافَ النَّهارِ) be a comitative object when it is a temporal adverb for the celebration with an implied "in," similar to the adverbial nature of "the hours of the night" (آناءِ اللَّيْلِ)?

I say: "The hours of the night" is not an adverb by its own wording—how could it be? It is preceded by "from" (من), and there is no meaning for an implied "in" (في) with it; rather, it points to the adverbial time. The meaning of "and during the hours of the night, celebrate" is "and during some hours of the night, celebrate therein." Let "and at the ends of the day" be likewise. The meaning is: "along with the ends of the day in which you celebrate," and the adverbial time in both cases is indicated and implied. This is the point.

So, if we say that "celebration" in the verse refers to something other than the obligatory prayers, the meaning would be celebration in parts of the beginning of the day, parts of its end, and parts of the night along with the parts of the beginning and end of the day. This would not entail the problem of repetition nor the problem of using a plural term for fewer than three, and this is clear.

If we say that "celebration" in the verse refers to the daily obligatory prayers, the verse would include the command for the Dawn prayer, the Afternoon prayer, and the Sunset and Night prayers only—similar to the command in His saying: **"Maintain the prayer at the two ends of the day, and during the early hours of the night"** (11:114). Perhaps the expression for the two times in the verse under discussion as "ends of the day" is to point to the expansiveness of the two times.

There is no harm in the verse containing four of the five daily prayers, for the Surah—as we shall point out—is among the earliest Surahs revealed in Mecca. The widely transmitted reports (الأخبار المستفيضة) narrated by both the general and specific [traditionists] indicate that the daily prayers were only prescribed as five during the Ascension (المعراج), as they were mentioned as five in Surah al-Isra—revealed after the Ascension—in His saying: **"Maintain the prayer from the sun's decline till the darkness of the night, and [observe particularly] the dawn recital"** (17:78). Perhaps those prescribed of the daily prayers at the time of the revelation of Surah Ta-Ha and Surah Hud—both of which were revealed before Surah al-Isra—were these four, and the Noon prayer had not yet been prescribed; indeed, this is the apparent meaning of the two verses: the verse of Ta-Ha and the verse of Hud.

It is known that this view is not subject to the objections raised against the view that "celebration" refers to the five prayers and the application of "ends of the day" to the time of the Noon prayer, which is the middle of the day. This is the point.

His saying: **"that you may be pleased (لَعَلَّكَ تَرْضى)."** The preceding context—which mentioned their turning away from the remembrance of their Lord, their forgetting His signs, their excess in their affairs, and their lack of faith, then mentioned the delay of vengeance upon them and commanded him to be patient, celebrate, and praise—requires that "pleasure" (الرضا) here means being pleased with the decree (قضاء) and destiny (قدر) of Allah. The meaning is: So be patient and celebrate the praise of your Lord so that pleasure with what Allah, the Glorified, has decreed may be achieved for you. Thus, it returns to a meaning similar to His saying: **"And take recourse in patience and prayer"** (2:45).

The reason for this is that the repeated remembrance of Him, the Exalted, by sanctifying His acts from deficiency and blemish, and remembering Him with beautiful praise and persisting in that, necessitates the soul's intimacy with Him and its increase. The increase of intimacy with the beauty of His act and its sanctity necessitates its firm rooting in the soul and its appearance in its vision, and the removal of the disturbing thoughts that confuse perception and thought. The soul is naturally disposed to be pleased with what it loves, and it loves nothing but the beautiful which is sanctified from ugliness and blemish. Thus, persisting in His remembrance through celebration and praise bequeaths pleasure with His decree.

It was said: "that you may be pleased" refers to intercession (الشفاعة) and the high rank with Allah. It was also said: "that you may be pleased" with all that Allah has promised you of victory and the honoring of the religion in this world, and intercession and Paradise in the Hereafter.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **"Do not extend your glance toward what We have provided certain groups of them as enjoyment—the splendor of the life of this world—that We may test them thereby..."** (20:131) etc. **Extending the eye (مد العين)** is to stretch its gaze and prolong it; this contains a rational metaphor (مجاز عقلي). Then, stretching the gaze and prolonging it toward something is an allusion (كناية) to being attached to it and loving it. What is meant by "groups" (أزواجاً)—as has been said—are the categories of disbelievers, or pairs of women and men among them, which refers back to households. The indefiniteness of "groups" is for belittlement and to show that they are of no consequence.

His saying: **"the splendor of the life of this world (زَهْرَةَ الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا)"** is in the position of an explanation for His saying: "what We have provided... as enjoyment." It is in the accusative case due to an implied verb, the underlying structure being: "We mean by it—or We have made for them—the splendor of the life of this world," which is its ornament and beauty. **Trial (الفتنة)** is examination and testing. It was said: what is meant by it is punishment, because the abundance of wealth and children is a type of punishment from Allah for them, as He said: **"So let not their wealth and their children impress you: Allah only desires to punish them with these in the life of this world, and that their souls should depart while they are faithless"** (9:85).

His saying: **"and the provision of your Lord is better and more lasting"** refers—by the evidence of its contrast with the splendor of the life of this world they were given—to the provision of the Hereafter, which is better and more lasting.

The meaning is: Do not prolong your gaze at the ornament of the life of this world and its beauty which We have given as enjoyment to certain categories or a limited number of pairs among them, in order to test them in what We have provided. That which your Lord will provide you in the Hereafter is better and more lasting.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **"And bid your family to prayer, and be steadfast in it. We do not ask any provision of you; it is We who provide for you, and the outcome is for godwariness."** (20:132). The verse has a context that harmonizes with the context of the other verses of the Surah; thus, it is Meccan like the rest, especially since we have not found anyone who excludes it and considers it Medinan. Accordingly, what is meant by **"your family" (أَهْلَكَ)**—according to its application at the time of revelation—is Khadijah, the wife of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله وسلم), and Ali (عليه السلام), for he was of his family and in his house; or these two and some of the daughters of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله وسلم).

Thus, the saying of some that it refers to his wives, his daughters, and his son-in-law Ali; and the saying of others that it refers to his wives, his daughters, and his relatives from the Banu Hashim and al-Muttalib; and the saying of others that it refers to all his followers from his nation—none of these are sound. Yes, there is no harm in the first saying in terms of the verse's general application and relevance, but not in terms of the specific occasion of revelation, for the verse is Meccan and he (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله وسلم) had no wife in Mecca other than Khadijah (عليه السلام).

His saying: **"We do not ask any provision of you; it is We who provide for you."** The apparent contrast between the two sentences is that what is meant is His, the Exalted, asking for provision for Himself, which is an allusion (كناية) for: "We are in no need of you, while you are the ones in need and impoverished before Us." Thus, it carries the meaning of His saying: **"I did not create the jinn and the humans except that they may worship Me. I do not desire any provision from them, nor do I desire that they should feed Me. Indeed it is Allah who is the All-provider, Lord of all might, the Invincible"** (51:56-58). Also, by virtue of its conclusion with His saying: "and the outcome is for godwariness," it carries the meaning of His saying: **"It is not their flesh or their blood that reaches Allah. Rather, it is your godwariness that reaches Him"** (22:37). Therefore, their interpretation of "asking for provision" as asking for provision for the creation or for the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله وسلم) himself is not sound.

His saying: **"and the outcome is for godwariness (وَالْعاقِبَةُ لِلتَّقْوى)"**—the discussion regarding this has been repeated many times.

It is not unlikely to derive from the verse—due to the restriction of the command for prayer to his family, along with what was in the two previous verses of the command to him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله وسلم) regarding himself for the four daily prayers and patience, and the prohibition against extending his eyes toward what the disbelievers were given—that the Surah was revealed in the early days of the Mission (البعثة), or specifically this verse. In what was narrated from Ibn Mas'ud, Surah Ta-Ha is among the "early ancient ones" (العتاق الأول). (1)

His saying: **"And they say, ‘Why does he not bring us a sign from his Lord?’ Has there not come to them the clarification of what is in the former scriptures?"** (20:133). This is a narration of the speech of the polytheists of Mecca. They only said this as an insinuation against the Quran, implying that it is not a sign indicating prophethood: "So let him bring us a sign just as the former ones were sent." "Clarification" (البينة) is the clear witness or that which is clear; it was also said it is the "explanation" (البيان).

In any case, their saying: "Why does he not bring us a sign from his Lord?" is an incitement (تحضيض) motivated by the desire to demean the Quran and to challenge the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله وسلم) by suggesting another miraculous sign. His saying: "Has there not come to them the clarification..." etc., is a response to it. Its meaning, according to the first of the two meanings of "clarification," is: Has there not come to them a clarification and a witness that testifies to what is in the former scriptures—which are the Torah, the Gospel, and the rest of the Heavenly Books—of the truths of knowledge and laws, and clarifies them? This is the Quran, and it was brought by a man who had no acquaintance with a teacher to teach him nor a prompter to prompt him with that.

According to the second meaning: Has there not come to them the explanation of what is in the former scriptures regarding the news of the past nations who suggested miraculous signs to their prophets, so they were brought, and their bringing was a cause for their destruction and extirpation when they did not believe in them after they had come? Why then do they not cease suggesting a sign after the Quran? Each of the two meanings has a parallel in His, the Exalted, speech.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **"Had We destroyed them with a punishment before it, they would have said, ‘Our Lord! Why did You not send us a messenger so that we might have followed Your signs before we were abased and humiliated?’"** (20:134). It appears that the pronoun in "before it" (مِنْ قَبْلِهِ) refers to the "clarification" (البينة)—in the previous verse—considering it to be the Quran. The meaning is: Had We destroyed them for their excess and disbelief with a punishment before the clarification came to them, the proof (الحجة) would not have been completed against them, and the proof would have been theirs against Us. They would have said: "Our Lord! Why did You not send us a messenger so that we might have followed Your signs"—which are those indicated by the clarification—"before we were abased" by the punishment of extirpation "and humiliated?"

It was said the pronoun refers to the "Messenger" known from the content of the previous verse, as evidenced by their saying: "Why did You not send us a messenger?" This is close in terms of wording, but the first meaning is closer in terms of sense, and it is supported by His saying: "so that we might have followed Your signs," and He did not say: "so that we might have followed Your messenger."

**His saying, the Exalted:** **"Say, ‘Everyone is waiting; so wait! Soon you will know who are the followers of the even path, and who is guided.’"** (20:135). **Waiting (التربص)** is expectation. **The even path (الصراط السوي)** is the straight path. His saying: "Everyone is waiting" means: each of us and you is waiting and expecting. We are waiting for what Allah has promised us regarding you and regarding the advancement of His religion and the completion of His light; and you are waiting for calamities to befall us so that you may nullify the true call. Each of us and you is following a path toward his objective. "So wait and expect"—and in this is a threat—"for soon you will know" which group among us and you are the followers of the straight path that leads to its objective, and who are those who have been guided to the objective. In this is an epic (ملحمة) and news of victory.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *Tafsir al-Qummi*: Regarding His saying, the Exalted: **"it would have been inevitable, and [for] a term already set,"** he said: "The punishment would have descended upon them, but He has delayed them until a set term."

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*: al-Tabarani, Ibn Mardawayh, and Ibn ‘Asakir narrated from Jarir, from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله وسلم) regarding His saying: **"and celebrate the praise of your Lord before the rising of the sun and before its setting"**—he said: "Before the rising of the sun is the Dawn prayer, and before its setting is the Afternoon prayer."

In *Tafsir al-Qummi*: Regarding His saying, the Exalted: **"and during the hours of the night, celebrate [His praise] and at the ends of the day"**—he said: "In the morning and the evening."

**I say:** This supports what we have previously presented.

In *al-Kafi*: With its chain of narration from Zurarah, from Abu Ja'far (عليه السلام), he said: I said to him: **"and at the ends of the day, that you may be pleased"**? He said: "It means voluntary prayers (تطوع) during the day."

**I say:** This is based on interpreting "celebration" as absolute prayer or absolute glorification.

In *Tafsir al-Qummi*: Regarding His saying, the Exalted: **"Do not extend your glance..."** the verse: Abu ‘Abdillah (عليه السلام) said: "When this verse was revealed, the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله وسلم) sat up straight, then said: 'Whoever does not find solace in the solace of Allah, his soul will be torn apart by regrets over the world. Whoever follows with his eyes what is in the hands of people, his grief will be prolonged and his rage will not be healed. And whoever does not recognize that Allah has a blessing upon him—neither in food nor in drink—his term will be shortened and his punishment will draw near.'"

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*: Ibn Abi Shaybah, Ibn Rahawayh, al-Bazzar, Abu Ya'la, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, Ibn Mardawayh, al-Khara'iti in *Makarim al-Akhlaq*, and Abu Nu'aym in *al-Ma'rifah* narrated from Abu Rafi', who said: A guest came to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله وسلم) and the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله وسلم) had nothing to host him with. So he sent me to a man from the Jews, saying: "Sell us—or lend us—some flour until the crescent of Rajab." He said: "No, except with a pledge (رهن)."

So I came to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله وسلم) and he said: "By Allah, I am indeed trustworthy in the heavens and trustworthy on the earth. Had he lent to me or sold to me, I would have paid him back. Go with my iron coat of mail." I had not left his presence until this verse was revealed: **"Do not extend your glance toward what We have provided certain groups of them as enjoyment..."** as if He were consoling him regarding the world.

**I say:** The content of the verse, and especially its conclusion, does not align with this story.

In it [al-Durr al-Manthur]: Ibn Mardawayh, Ibn ‘Asakir, and Ibn al-Najjar narrated from Abu Sa'id al-Khudri, who said: "When **'And bid your family to prayer'** was revealed, the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله وسلم) used to come to the door of Ali for eight months, saying: 'The prayer, may Allah have mercy on you! *Allah only desires to remove all impurity from you, O People of the House, and to purify you with a thorough purification* (33:33).'"

**I say:** It was also narrated in *Majma' al-Bayan* from al-Khudri, and in it "nine months" is mentioned instead of "eight months." This meaning was also narrated in *al-Uyun*, in the gathering of al-Rida with al-Ma'mun, from him (عليه السلام). al-Qummi also narrated it in his *Tafsir* as a *marfu‘* report. The specification of nine months is based on what the narrator witnessed, not on a limitation of the actual frequency of his (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله وسلم) coming. The evidence for this is what al-Shaykh narrated in *al-Amali*, with his chain from Abu al-Humayra', who said: "I witnessed the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله وسلم) for forty mornings coming to the door of Ali and Fatimah, taking hold of the two doorposts, then saying: 'Peace be upon you, O People of the House, and the mercy of Allah and His blessings. The prayer, may Allah have mercy on you! *Allah only desires to remove all impurity from you, O People of the House, and to purify you with a thorough purification*.'"

The apparent meaning of the narration is that the verse is Medinan, yet no one has mentioned that as far as I recall. Perhaps what is meant is to clarify his (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله وسلم) coming to the door in Medina in practice of the verse, even if it had been revealed in Mecca, though this is far from the literal wording. In the narration of al-Qummi to which we alluded, there is support for this meaning, for it says: "He did not cease doing that every day when he was present in Medina until he departed from the world." The report of his commanding his household to prayer is also narrated through other paths besides those already mentioned.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*: Abu ‘Ubayd, Sa'id ibn Mansur, Ibn al-Mundhir, al-Tabarani in *al-Awsat*, Abu Nu'aym in *al-Hilyah*, and al-Bayhaqi in *Shu'ab al-Iman* narrated with a sound chain from ‘Abdullah ibn Salam, who said: "Whenever a hardship or distress befell the family of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله وسلم), he would command them to pray and recite the verse: **'And bid your family to prayer...'**"

**I say:** This meaning was also narrated from Ahmad in *al-Zuhd*, Ibn Abi Hatim, and al-Bayhaqi in *Shu'ab al-Iman*, from Thabit from him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله وسلم). In this is an indication of the expansiveness in the meaning of "celebration" in the verse.
